items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: During boiling the temperature of a pure liquid:
items.why: It's stays constant. “rises steadily” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Pencil is used for chromatography baseline because:
items.random_wrong: it is required by exam boards
items.why: The answer examiners want is: pencil graphite does not dissolve. “it is darker” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Energy taken in to melt 50 g of ice (latent heat 334 J/g) in kJ?
items.why: Exact answer: 16.7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
